coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of high-performance hydrophilic fluorescent carbon dots derived from flowering plum and their application for trace detection of chlortetracycline
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Meiling
Wu, Shaogui
coredata.dc:description: The residual chlortetracycline (CTC) in food environments represents a potential risk to human health and the ecological environment, rendering its trace detection technology a focal point of current research. In this study, high-performance hydrophilic blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were successfully synthesized using Flowering plum as the carbon source and anhydrous sulfanilic acid as the nitrogen source via a one-step hydrothermal method. These carbon dots display an emission wavelength of 477 nm and a high quantum yield of 35 %. The B-CDs demonstrate exceptional photoluminescent stability, with minimal influence from temperature, UV irradiation, and storage time. Furthermore, the presence of CTC induces fluorescence quenching in the B-CDs. Leveraging the specific response of B-CDs to CTC, we developed a fluorescent probe for the detection of trace amounts of CTC. This probe demonstrates a strong linear relationship within the detection concentration range of 6 to 13 μM, with a detection limit as low as 0.21 μM. It has also been successfully applied to the detection of CTC in real water samples, achieving a recovery rate of 99.22 %–101.74 %. Consequently, this study presents a promising novel approach for the detection of trace CTC, offering substantial reference value.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Chlortetracycline
Fluorescence probe

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Detection of CTC in real water samples

xocs:cp-notices.xocs:cp-notice: © 2025 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic Diagram of the Synthesis of B-CDs.

Structural characterization of B-CDs. (a) TEM image; inset: HRTEM image. (b) XRD pattern. (c) FTIR spectrum. (d) XPS survey spectrum and high-resolution spectra of C 1s (e), N 1s (f), O 1s (g), and S 2p (h).

Optical properties of B-CDs. (a) Photographs of B-CDs Under Diverse Illumination Conditions. (b) Two-dimensional projection of the excitation-emission matrix (EEM). (c) UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra. (d) CIE chromaticity diagram. (e) Normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths and (f) non-normalized emission spectra.

Detection of CTC Using B-CDs. (a) Selectivity of B-CDs toward CTC. (b) Sensitivity of B-CDs to CTC. (c) Linear fitting of B-CDs fluorescence intensity versus CTC concentration. (d) UV–Vis absorption spectra of B-CDs, CTC, and the B-CDs–CTC complex. (e) Anti-interference experiment. (f) IR spectra of B-CDs before and after the addition of CTC. (g) Fluorescence lifetimes of B-CDs before and after the addition of CTC. (h) UV–Vis absorption spectrum of CTC (green) and excitation (orange) and emission (yellow) spectra of B-CDs. (For interpretation of the references to colour in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the web version of this article.)
Determination of CTC content in tap water and pond water.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of high-performance hydrophilic fluorescent carbon dots derived from flowering plum and their application for trace detection of chlortetracycline

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The residual chlortetracycline (CTC) in food environments represents a potential risk to human health and the ecological environment, rendering its trace detection technology a focal point of current research. In this study, high-performance hydrophilic blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were successfully synthesized using Flowering plum as the carbon source and anhydrous sulfanilic acid as the nitrogen source via a one-step hydrothermal method. These carbon dots display an emission wavelength of 477 nm and a high quantum yield of 35 %. The B-CDs demonstrate exceptional photoluminescent stability, with minimal influence from temperature, UV irradiation, and storage time. Furthermore, the presence of CTC induces fluorescence quenching in the B-CDs. Leveraging the specific response of B-CDs to CTC, we developed a fluorescent probe for the detection of trace amounts of CTC. This probe demonstrates a strong linear relationship within the detection concentration range of 6 to 13 μM, with a detection limit as low as 0.21 μM. It has also been successfully applied to the detection of CTC in real water samples, achieving a recovery rate of 99.22 %–101.74 %. Consequently, this study presents a promising novel approach for the detection of trace CTC, offering substantial reference value.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) are a class of discrete, quasi-spherical fluorescent carbon nanomaterials with diameters typically less than 10 nm. Since their discovery by Xu et al. in 2004 [1], CDs have attracted significant research attention over the past two decades [2,3]. Various precursors, including molecular compounds [4], polymers [5], and biomass [6,7], have been employed for CDs synthesis. Among these, Flowering plum, a natural biomass resource with high carbon content, sustainability, and environmental friendliness, has emerged as an ideal precursor for CDs synthesis. CDs exhibit remarkable fluorescence properties, emitting strong fluorescence upon excitation at specific wavelengths while maintaining exceptional photostability with minimal environmental interference [8].

This unique fluorescence property endows CDs with great potential for applications in various fields, including bioimaging [9,10], chemical sensing [11–13], and environmental pollution control [14–16]. In modern society, antibiotics play a crucial role in medical, environmental, and agricultural fields. However, the excessive use of antibiotics has raised serious concerns regarding ecological balance, human health, and food safety [17]. Consequently, the accurate detection of antibiotics in various matrices has become imperative. Chlortetracycline (CTC), a broad-spectrum antibiotic widely used in medical and veterinary applications, is of particular interest due to its potential environmental accumulation. When interacting with CDs, CTC induces fluorescence quenching through specific mechanisms, offering a novel approach for its quantitative analysis. Given the high sensitivity of CDs to CTC, they can be rationally designed as efficient fluorescence probes for the rapid and accurate detection of CTC content [18].

Current CTC detection methods include liquid chromatography–mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS), enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay (ELISA) [19], and capillary electrophoresis [20]. Additionally, electrochemical analysis has been applied for CTC detection [21,22]. While these techniques offer high sensitivity and accuracy, they often require complex sample preparation, skilled personnel, costly instrumentation, and extended analysis times. In contrast, fluorescence-based detection offers advantages such as simplicity, high sensitivity, rapid analysis, and suitability for in vivo applications [23].

In this study, nitrogen- and sulfur-co-doped blue fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a green hydrothermal method using Flowering plum and p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid as precursors. The resulting B-CDs exhibited excellent fluorescence properties, with a quantum yield of 35 % and an emission wavelength of 477 nm. Moreover, B-CDs demonstrated high sensitivity towards CTC, highlighting their potential for practical applications in trace detection of CTC in environmental and food samples.

Instruments: 756PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer (Shanghai Shunyuhengping Co., Ltd.) and F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shanghai Longguang Co., Ltd.). X-ray diffractometer (XRD, D-Max 2550), high-resolution transmission electron microscope (HRTEM, JEOL Ltd.), X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS, Kratos AXIS Ultra DLD), and Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (FTIR, VERIEX 70).

Reagents: Flowering plum samples were collected from the university campus. Anhydrous ethanol (EtOH), p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid, acetonitrile (ACN), tetrahydrofuran (THF), acetone (ACE), dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO), and N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) were purchased from Chengdu Cologne Chemicals Co., Ltd. Chloramphenicol and ampicillin sodium were obtained from Hefei Bomei Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Ceftriaxone 3.5-hydrate, oxytetracycline, erythromycin, streptomycin sulfate, and chlortetracycline hydrochloride were supplied by Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Amoxicillin, ceftazidime hydrochloride, and tetracycline were acquired from Shanghai Macklin Biotechnology Co., Ltd., and gentamicin was sourced from Nanjing Dulai Biotechnology Co., Ltd. Distilled water was provided by the university’s water purification facility. All reagents used were of analytical reagent (AR) grade.

Fluorescent plum-derived carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method. Briefly, 2 mL of flowering plum extract (obtained through ethanol extraction) was employed as the carbon source, and 0.2 g of p-aminobenzenesulfonic acid served as the nitrogen source. These precursors were dissolved in 10 mL of distilled water and ultrasonicated for 10 min (F-009SD ultrasonic cleaner) to ensure homogeneous mixing. The resulting mixture was then transferred into a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and heated at 180 °C for 6 h. After cooling to room temperature, the crude product was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 15 min to remove large aggregates or unreacted particles. Finally, the supernatant was purified using column chromatography to obtain a pale-yellow carbon dot solution. Fig. 1 illustrates the entire synthesis workflow, detailing each step from precursor preparation to product purification.

The quantum yield (ϕ) of B-CDs was determined using quinine sulfate as a reference standard [24]. A series of quinine sulfate and B-CDs solutions were prepared, and their absorbance was measured at the maximum excitation wavelength using a UV–Vis spectrophotometer. The absorbance was maintained at approximately 0.05 to minimize measurement errors. The fluorescence emission spectra of these solutions were recorded using a fluorescence spectrophotometer. The quantum yield was calculated using the following equation:
(1)
φ =
φ s
·
( K /
K s
)
·
( η /
η s
)
2
where φ and φ s represent the quantum yields of the sample and quinine sulfate solution, respectively; K and K s denote the slopes of the fluorescence intensity versus absorbance curves for the sample and quinine sulfate, respectively; and η and η s are the refractive indices of the sample and quinine sulfate solutions, respectively.

The selective detection of CTC was conducted at room temperature. Initially, solutions of CTC and various other antibiotics were prepared at a concentration of 5 mM under identical experimental conditions. Subsequently, 2 mL of distilled water and 20 μL of B-CDs solution were added sequentially to a 5 mL cuvette, thoroughly mixed, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 353 nm. The fluorescence intensity at the maximum emission wavelength was taken as the blank control. Following this, 2 mL of distilled water, 20 μL of B-CDs solution, and 50 μL of CTC solution (or other antibiotic solutions) were added to the same cuvette, mixed thoroughly, and their fluorescence spectra were recorded using the same procedure. The results demonstrated that B-CDs exhibited high selectivity for CTC detection when compared under identical conditions with other antibiotics.

To further investigate the sensitivity of B-CDs towards CTC, a 200 μM CTC solution was prepared. Then, 0 μL-140 μL of CTC solution were separately mixed with 2 mL of distilled water and 20 μL of B-CDs solution. The corresponding fluorescence spectra were recorded at an excitation wavelength of 353 nm. All measurements were repeated three times to ensure data accuracy and reliability. In the subsequent anti-interference experiments, the concentration of the prepared chlortetracycline (CTC) solution and related antibiotic solutions was 5 mM, while the concentration of the relevant metal ion solutions was 20 μM. The testing volume consisted of 2 mL of distilled water and 50 μL of the interfering substance solution (plus 50 μL of CTC solution, where applicable), and the testing procedure followed the same steps as previously described.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of CTC in real water samples
ce:section.ce:para: The real water samples included tap water and pond water. Tap water was obtained directly from the laboratory. Pond water was collected from a campus pond, filtered using a 0.22 μm aqueous filter membrane, and centrifuged at 10,000 rpm for 5 min to remove solid particles and impurities.

Detection procedure: In a 5 mL cuvette, 2 mL of real water sample, 20 μL of B-CDs solution, and CTC solutions with concentrations of 6 μM, 8 μM, and 12 μM (spiked concentrations) were added. The fluorescence spectra were recorded under an excitation wavelength of 353 nm, and the maximum fluorescence intensity (F) was measured. The value of y was calculated using the equation y = 1 − F/F0 (where F0 is the maximum fluorescence intensity of the blank sample), and then substituted into the linear regression equation y = 0.03348x + 0.39652 to obtain x (the recovered concentration). The recovery rate was calculated as (recovered value/spiked value) × 100 %, and the relative standard deviation (RSD) was determined from three parallel experiments.

The structure of B-CDs was characterized using transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier-transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy, and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The TEM image (Fig. 2 a) reveals that B-CDs exhibit a granular morphology with an average particle size of approximately 5 nm. The high-resolution TEM (HRTEM) image shows a lattice spacing of 0.21 nm, which corresponds to the (100) plane of graphene [25]. The X-ray diffraction (XRD) pattern (Fig. 2b) displays a strong diffraction peak at 21.12°, associated with the (002) plane of graphite [26].

The FTIR spectrum (Fig. 2c) was employed to analyze the surface functional groups of B-CDs. The characteristic peak at 3449 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O-H/N-H bonds [27], while the peak at 2929 cm−1 is attributed to C-H stretching vibrations. A weak absorption peak at 2590 cm−1 is assigned to the stretching vibrations of S-H bonds [28]. The peak at 2360 cm−1 corresponds to the stretching vibrations of O=C=O bonds, while those at 1642 cm−1 and 1495 cm−1 are ascribed to C=O/C=N and C=C stretching vibrations, respectively [29]. A strong absorption peak at 1208 cm−1 is associated with C-N stretching vibrations, while peaks at 1055 cm−1 and 745 cm−1 are attributed to C-O and C-S stretching vibrations, respectively [30]. To further elucidate the surface composition of B-CDs, XPS analysis was performed (Fig. 2d). Distinct characteristic peaks corresponding to S-2p, C-1 s, N-1 s, and O-1 s were observed at binding energies of 172.98, 295.98, 407.98, and 542.98 eV, with atomic compositions of 5.42 %, 62.05 %, 3.8 %, and 28.72 %, respectively. This confirms the successful doping of N and S elements into the B-CDs. Deconvolution of the high-resolution XPS spectra (Fig. 2e) further supports these findings. The C-1 s spectrum reveals three peaks at 288.08, 286.59, and 284.80 eV, corresponding to C=O, C-N/C-O/C-S, and C-C/C=C bonds, respectively [31]. The N-1s spectrum is deconvoluted into two peaks at 403.62 eV and 401.89 eV, representing graphitic N and pyrrolic N (Fig. 2f) [29]. In the O-1s spectrum, two peaks at 534.48 and 532.31 eV correspond to C-O and C=O bonds (Fig. 2g) [32]. The S-2p spectrum exhibits two peaks at 170.51 eV and 168.34 eV, which are attributed to S-H and S-C bonds, respectively (Fig. 2h) [33].

The optical properties of B-CDs were systematically investigated through excitation-emission matrix (EEM), UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy, fluorescence emission spectroscopy, and excitation spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 3 a, B-CDs emitted bright blue fluorescence under UV irradiation, with EEM projections revealing maximum excitation and emission wavelengths at approximately 355 nm and 475 nm, respectively (Fig. 3b). To further validate these observations, Fig. 3c presents the UV–Vis absorption, excitation, and emission spectra of B-CDs. The UV–Vis spectrum exhibits a strong absorption peak at 241 nm, attributed to π → π* transitions of C=C bonds, while weaker absorption peaks at 304 nm and 353 nm correspond to n → π* transitions of C=N and π → π* transitions of C=S bonds, respectively. The excitation spectrum displays dual peaks at 353 nm and 404 nm, indicating the presence of complex emissive centers within B-CDs. When excited at 353 nm, B-CDs demonstrate blue fluorescence with a maximum emission wavelength of 477 nm, as confirmed by the CIE chromaticity diagram in Fig. 3d. Additionally, Fig. 3e and 3f display the emission spectra of B-CDs under varying excitation wavelengths. Within the excitation range of 330–380 nm, the maximum emission wavelength remains constant, suggesting that the emission properties of B-CDs are independent of both surface state theory and quantum size effects, instead exhibiting excitation wavelength-independent behavior [25,34].

The quantum yield (QY) of B-CDs was evaluated using quinine sulfate as a reference, examining the influence of different solvents (Fig. S1a). B-CDs demonstrated the highest QY in water at 35.8 %, whereas a significant decrease to 5.0 % was observed in DMF. In other solvents, including ACN, ACE, EtOH, and THF, the QY ranged from 10 % to 30 %. These findings highlight the substantial impact of the solvent environment on the fluorescence performance of B-CDs. Specifically, B-CDs exhibited enhanced fluorescence in aqueous environments, whereas fluorescence was significantly quenched in DMF. The variations in QY among different solvents can be attributed to the combined effects of solvent physicochemical properties, the intrinsic characteristics of B-CDs, and complex solvent-CDs interactions.

To evaluate the stability of B-CDs, we systematically investigated their fluorescence intensity under varying conditions. Fig. S2a demonstrates that B-CDs maintained relatively stable fluorescence across a broad pH range of 3–11. However, significant fluorescence quenching occurred under highly acidic (pH < 3) or strongly alkaline (pH > 11) conditions. Notably, at extreme pH values of 12 and 13, a distinct blue shift in the maximum emission wavelength was observed, decreasing from 477 nm to 438 nm. This spectral shift is likely caused by deprotonation of surface amino and thiol groups in high-pH environments, altering the electronic structure of the emissive centers. Thermal stability analysis (Fig. S2b) revealed that fluorescence intensity decreased marginally between 10–70 °C but remained generally stable across this temperature range. In the salt tolerance test, it was found that the fluorescence intensity of B-CDs was significantly affected by the concentration of NaCl solution—the higher the concentration, the more pronounced the decrease in fluorescence (Fig. S2c). Subsequent photostability testing under continuous UV irradiation (Fig. S2d) showed no significant change in fluorescence intensity after 120 min of exposure, confirming exceptional resistance to photobleaching. Finally, storage stability assessment over 14 days demonstrated nearly constant fluorescence intensity (Fig. S2e), highlighting the superior long-term stability of B-CDs under ambient conditions.

Fluorescent carbon dots (CDs) have been widely utilized as efficient fluorescent probes for detecting various antibiotics. As illustrated in Fig. 4 a, we systematically investigated the selective fluorescence response of B-CDs toward different antibiotics, including tetracycline (TC), penicillin G (PG), ceftriaxone (FEP), chlortetracycline (CTC), amoxicillin (AMC), oxytetracycline (OTC), ceftazidime (CAZ), chloramphenicol (CPL), erythromycin (EM), streptomycin (SM), and gentamicin (GM). The results demonstrated that B-CDs exhibited outstanding selectivity toward CTC, making them a promising candidate for CTC detection. To further assess the sensitivity of B-CDs toward CTC, fluorescence quenching experiments were conducted by gradually increasing the CTC concentration. As shown in Fig. 4b, fluorescence intensity decreased progressively with increasing CTC concentration in the range of 0–14 μM, with a strong linear relationship observed between 6 and 13 μM. The corresponding linear equation is 0.03348x + 0.39652, with an R-squared value of 0.99447 (Fig. 4c). Furthermore, the sensitivity of the detection method was further validated by introducing typical antibiotics and common metal ions, as shown in Fig. 4d. Even in the presence of various antibiotics and metal ions, B-CDs maintained high selectivity toward CTC, demonstrating excellent anti-interference capability. Compared to other reported methods for CTC detection, the B-CDs synthesized in this study exhibited a remarkably low detection limit of 0.21 μM. In addition, they feature a simple preparation process, low cost, and achieved better recovery rates in subsequent real water sample tests, highlighting their potential for trace-level CTC detection (Table S1).

UV–Vis absorption spectroscopy was employed to investigate the interaction mechanism between B-CDs and CTC. As shown in Fig. 4e, the absorption peak at 353 nm exhibited a significant enhancement upon the addition of CTC. Moreover, in the infrared spectrum, the O–H/N–H stretching vibration peak at 3449 cm−1 weakened, the S–H stretching vibration peak at 2590 cm−1 disappeared, and the intensities of various functional group absorption peaks in the range of 745–1600 cm−1 were markedly reduced (Fig. 4f). These observations confirm that specific binding sites within the molecular structure of CTC interact with B-CDs, forming a stable complex that leads to fluorescence quenching. Consistent with this observation, Fig. 4g demonstrates no significant change in B-CDs' fluorescence lifetime after CTC addition, confirming the formation of non-radiative complexes rather than dynamic collision-induced quenching. Further mechanistic insights are provided in Fig. 4h, which reveals substantial spectral overlap between CTC's UV absorption spectrum and B-CDs' excitation spectrum. This overlap indicates that the inner filter effect (IFE) may also contribute to the observed fluorescence quenching. Collectively, these findings demonstrate that the fluorescence quenching mechanism during CTC detection involves synergistic contributions from both static quenching (complex formation) and inner filter effects, with the latter likely enhancing the former through spectral overlap-induced excitation energy redistribution.

To validate the analytical feasibility of the proposed method, spike-and-recovery experiments for CTC were conducted in tap water and pond water samples. As presented in Table 1 , the obtained recovery rates ranged from 99.22 % to 101.74 %, with relative standard deviations (RSDs) between 0.13 % and 1.96 %. These results demonstrate the high accuracy and precision of the B-CDs-based detection system, confirming its practical applicability for CTC quantitation in authentic environmental samples. The consistent recovery values across the tested concentration range further substantiate the reliability of this nanoprobe-based analytical approach for real-world applications.

In this study, blue-emitting fluorescent carbon dots (B-CDs) were synthesized via a one-step hydrothermal method using Flowering plum and anhydrous p-toluenesulfonic acid as precursors. Structural characterizations using TEM, XRD, FTIR, and XPS confirmed the presence of a graphitized carbon core and the functional groups on the B-CDs' surface. Optical analysis demonstrated that B-CDs exhibit excitation-independent emission properties. Furthermore, the effects of environmental factors, including pH, temperature, salt concentration, UV irradiation, and placement time on B-CDs fluorescence were evaluated. The results showed that the fluorescence of B-CDs remained stable under varying temperatures and UV irradiation. Moreover, the fluorescence intensity remained nearly unchanged after storage for up to 14 days. However, significant quenching was observed under extreme pH conditions and in high-concentration NaCl environments. Finally, leveraging the high selectivity of B-CDs toward CTC, we developed a fluorescence-based sensing platform for trace-level CTC detection. The probe demonstrated an ultralow detection limit of 0.21 μM and achieved excellent recovery rates in real water samples such as tap water and pond water, providing a novel and efficient approach for CTC detection with significant scientific and practical implications.
Meiling Long: Writing – original draft, Investigation, Formal analysis. Shaogui Wu: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2025.114187.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary Data 1

J. Am. Chem. Soc.
